'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらののおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]（旧・[[清水町 (和歌山県)|清水町]]杉野原）に伝わる民俗芸能の御田舞。
[[1987年]][[12月28日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[花園の御田舞]]
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:和歌山県|すきのはらののおんたまい]] [[category:日本の文化|すきのはらののおんたまい]] [[Category:伝統芸能|すきのはらののおんたまい]]

'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらののおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]（旧・[[清水町 (和歌山県)|清水町]]杉野原）に伝わる民俗芸能の御田舞。
[[1987年]][[12月28日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[花園の御田舞]]
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:和歌山県|すきのはらののおんたまい]] [[Category:伝統芸能|すきのはらののおんたまい]]

'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらのおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]（旧・[[清水町 (和歌山県)|清水町]]杉野原）に伝わる民俗芸能の御田舞。
[[1987年]][[12月28日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[花園の御田舞]]
{{japan-culture-stub}} [[Category:和歌山県|すきのはらのおんたまい]] [[Category:伝統芸能|すきのはらのおんたまい]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財|すきのはらのおんたまい]]

'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらのおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]（旧・[[清水町 (和歌山県)|清水町]]）杉野原に伝わる民俗芸能。[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
旧清水町は、[[高野山]]への[[街道]]や竜神往来など[[山岳]][[巡礼|遍路]]の[[宿場町]]として古くから栄え、多くの[[文化財]]や[[伝統]]的な民俗芸能を残しているところである。
御田舞は、古くは当川といわれた[[有田川]]沿いの9ヶ村に伝来した[[田楽]]から変化した農耕予祝[[行事]]であり、杉野原の御田舞は、中世からの舞殿がここにのみ残っている雨錫寺阿弥陀堂で、隔年の[[2月11日]]に公開される。春田打ちから籾摺りまでの20数通りの稲作の生産過程を、舅が婿に教えるとして演じものである。冒頭には、屈強な男達が[[褌]]姿で肩を組み、太鼓を打ち、唄囃子に合わせ揉み合い押し合いして踊る、勇壮な「裸苗押し」が展開される。
また、全国に伝承されている御田のほとんどが[[田植え]]で終わるといわれ、収穫までの全生産過程を演じる例は全国的にもめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]

また、全国に伝承されている[[御田]]のほとんどが[[田植]]で終わるといわれ、収穫までの全生産過程を演じる例は全国的にもめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。

'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらのおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]（旧・[[清水町 (和歌山県)|清水町]]）杉野原に伝わる民俗芸能。[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
旧清水町は、[[高野山]]への[[街道]]や竜神往来など[[山岳]][[巡礼|遍路]]の[[宿場町]]として古くから栄え、多くの[[文化財]]や[[伝統]]的な[[民俗]][[芸能]]を残しているところである。
御田舞は、古くは当川といわれた[[有田川]]沿いの9ヶ村に伝来した[[田楽]]から変化した[[農耕]][[予祝芸能|予祝行事]]であり、杉野原の御田舞は、[[中世#日本|中世]]からの舞殿がここにのみ残っている雨錫寺阿弥陀堂で、隔年の[[2月11日]]に公開される。春田打ちから籾摺りまでの20数通りの[[稲作]]の生産過程を、舅が婿に教えるとして演じものである。冒頭には、屈強な男達が[[褌]]姿で肩を組み、[[太鼓]]を打ち、唄囃子に合わせ揉み合い押し合いして舞う、勇壮な「裸苗押し」が展開される。
また、日本全国に[[伝承]]されている[[御田]]のほとんどが[[田植]]で終わるといわれ、[[収穫]]までの全生産過程を演じる例はめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。

また、日本全国に[[伝承]]されている[[御田]]のほとんどが[[田植]]で終わるといわれ、[[収穫]]までの全生産過程を演じる例はめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]
== 外部リンク == *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|112|杉野原の御田舞}} *[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞 - 文化遺産オンライン]

revision.text:
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arita District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (Japan)|en|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|en|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|en|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|en|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|en|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Traditional performing arts of Japan|en|伝統芸能}}
== External Links == * {{Cultural Heritage Online|200126|杉野原の御田舞}} * [https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞 - 文化遺産オンライン] * [https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018年2月号、有田川町役場

revision.text:
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arita District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]]

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]]

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}
== External Links == * {{Cultural Heritage Online|200126|杉野原の御田舞}} * [https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞 - 文化遺産オンライン] * [https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018年2月号、有田川町役場

[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]]

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

revision.text:
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

revision.text:
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}
== External Links == * {{Cultural Heritage Online|200126|杉野原の御田舞}} * [https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞 - 文化遺産オンライン] * [https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018年2月号、有田川町役場

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also! == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. '''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|ja|有田川町}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|ja|有田郡}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|post town|ja|宿場町}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|cultural properties of Japan|ja|文化財}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|ja|田楽}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|ja|2月11日}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|ja|かつらぎ町}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

revision.text:
'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらのおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]杉野原に伝わる民俗芸能。[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
町内では、[[高野山]]への[[街道]]や竜神往来など[[山岳]][[巡礼|遍路]]の[[宿場町]]として古くから栄え、多くの[[文化財]]や[[伝統]]的な[[民俗]][[芸能]]を残している。
御田舞は、古くは当川といわれた[[有田川]]沿いの9ヶ村に伝来した[[田楽]]から変化した[[農耕]][[予祝芸能|予祝行事]]であり、杉野原の御田舞は、[[中世#日本|中世]]からの舞殿がここにのみ残っている雨錫寺阿弥陀堂で、隔年の[[2月11日]]に公開される。春田打ちから籾摺りまでの20数通りの[[稲作]]の生産過程を、舅が婿に教えるとして演じものである。冒頭には、屈強な男たちが[[褌]]姿で肩を組み、[[太鼓]]を打ち、唄囃子に合わせ揉み合い押し合いして舞う、勇壮な「裸苗押し」が展開される。
また、日本全国に[[伝承]]されている[[御田]]のほとんどが[[田植]]で終わるといわれ、[[収穫]]までの全生産過程を演じる例はめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]
== 外部リンク == *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|112|杉野原の御田舞}} *[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞] - 文化遺産オンライン *[https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018年2月号、有田川町役場
{{Coord|34|7|4.03|N|135|30|14.5|E|region:JP-30_type:event_scale:10000|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すきのはらのおんたまい}} [[Category:和歌山県の祭り]] [[Category:有田川町]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[杉野原の御田舞]] to [[Suginohara Onta-mai]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''杉野原の御田舞'''（すぎのはらのおんだまい）は、[[和歌山県]][[有田郡]][[有田川町]]杉野原に伝わる民俗芸能。[[重要無形民俗文化財]]。
町内では、[[高野山]]への[[街道]]や竜神往来など[[山岳]][[巡礼|遍路]]の[[宿場町]]として古くから栄え、多くの[[文化財]]や[[伝統]]的な[[民俗]][[芸能]]を残している。
御田舞は、古くは当川といわれた[[有田川]]沿いの9ヶ村に伝来した[[田楽]]から変化した[[農耕]][[予祝芸能|予祝行事]]であり、杉野原の御田舞は、[[中世#日本|中世]]からの舞殿がここにのみ残っている雨錫寺阿弥陀堂で、隔年の[[2月11日]]に公開される。春田打ちから籾摺りまでの20数通りの[[稲作]]の生産過程を、舅が婿に教えるとして演じものである。冒頭には、屈強な男たちが[[褌]]姿で肩を組み、[[太鼓]]を打ち、唄囃子に合わせ揉み合い押し合いして舞う、勇壮な「裸苗押し」が展開される。
また、日本全国に[[伝承]]されている[[御田]]のほとんどが[[田植]]で終わるといわれ、[[収穫]]までの全生産過程を演じる例はめずらしく、[[かつらぎ町]]の[[花園の御田舞]]とともにこの地方特有の貴重なものである。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]]
== 外部リンク == *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|112|杉野原の御田舞}} *[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞] - 文化遺産オンライン *[https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018年2月号、有田川町役場
{{Coord|34|7|4.03|N|135|30|14.5|E|region:JP-30_type:event_scale:10000|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すきのはらのおんたまい}} [[Category:和歌山県の祭り]] [[Category:有田川町]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:予祝芸能]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11522359]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from [[:en:Aridagawa, Wakayama|Aridagawa, Wakayama]], [[:en:Arida District, Wakayama|Arida District, Wakayama]], in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

== External Links ==
* {{Cultural Heritage Online|200126|杉野原の御田舞}}
* [https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/200126 杉野原の御田舞 - 文化遺産オンライン]
* [https://www.town.aridagawa.lg.jp/material/files/group/11/koho2018-2_28.pdf シリーズ探訪 わが町文化財「久野原の御田が奉納されます」] - 広報ありだがわ2018th year 2月号、有田川町役場

It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also ==

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from [[:en:Aridagawa, Wakayama|Aridagawa, Wakayama]], [[:en:Arida District, Wakayama|Arida District, Wakayama]], in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11522359]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from [[:en:Aridagawa, Wakayama|Aridagawa, Wakayama]], [[:en:Arida District, Wakayama|Arida District, Wakayama]], in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.

Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region.
== See Also ==

Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11522359]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from [[:en:Aridagawa, Wakayama|Aridagawa, Wakayama]], [[:en:Arida District, Wakayama|Arida District, Wakayama]], in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:杉野原の御田舞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11522359]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from [[:en:Aridagawa, Wakayama|Aridagawa, Wakayama]], [[:en:Arida District, Wakayama|Arida District, Wakayama]], in [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]] and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many [[:en:Cultural Property (Japan)|cultural properties of Japan]] and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in [[:en:Katsuragi, Wakayama|Katsuragi, Wakayama]], it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

The Ota-mai, derived from [[:en:Dengaku|Dengaku]], evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on [[:en:February 11|February 11]], takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|en|February 11|WD=Q2335|lt=February 11}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|en|Katsuragi, Wakayama|WD=Q1345594|lt=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|en|Aridagawa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345889|lt=Aridagawa, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|en|Arida District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131181|lt=Arida District, Wakayama}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=cultural properties of Japan}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|en|February 11|WD=Q2335|lt=February 11}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|en|Katsuragi, Wakayama|WD=Q1345594|lt=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|en|Katsuragi, Wakayama|WD=Q1345594|lt=Katsuragi, Wakayama|1=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=Theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

'''Suginohara no Ota-mai''' (杉野原の御田舞) is a folk performance tradition from {{ill|Aridagawa, Wakayama|en|Aridagawa, Wakayama|WD=Q1345889|lt=Aridagawa, Wakayama|1=Aridagawa, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Arida District, Wakayama|en|Arida District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131181|lt=Arida District, Wakayama|1=Arida District, Wakayama}}, in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It has been designated an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.
In the town, historically significant as a {{ill|Shukuba|lt=Shukuba|lt=Post town|ja|宿場町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/post town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宿場}} on the routes to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}} and the Ryūjin pilgrimage paths, many {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=cultural properties of Japan|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} and traditional folk performances have been preserved.
The Ota-mai, derived from {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|WD=Q3022455|lt=Dengaku|1=Dengaku}}, evolved into a ritual celebrating agricultural abundance. It was practiced in nine villages along the {{ill|Aridagawa River|ja|有田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aridagawa River|qq=}}, formerly called Tokugawa. Suginohara’s Ota-mai, performed biennially on {{ill|February 11|en|February 11|WD=Q2335|lt=February 11|1=February 11}}, takes place at the {{ill|Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja|雨錫寺阿弥陀堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Hall of Amijaku-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the only remaining medieval-style stage in the region. The event reenacts over 20 steps of the rice production cycle, from plowing to milling, in a dramatized instructional exchange between a father-in-law and his son-in-law. The performance begins with the vigorous "Hadaka Nae-oshi" (裸苗押し), where robust men in loincloths lock arms, drum, and engage in energetic pushing and grappling to the rhythm of chants and music.
Suginohara no Ota-mai is notable for depicting the entire rice production process, including harvest, which is rare among {{ill|Ota (ritual)|ja|御田|1=Sacred rice field|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ota (ritual)|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred rice field|qq=}} performances in Japan, most of which conclude at the planting stage. Alongside the {{ill|Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono Onta-mai|lt=Hanazono no Ota-mai|ja|花園の御田舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanazono no Ota-mai|qq=}} in {{ill|Katsuragi, Wakayama|en|Katsuragi, Wakayama|WD=Q1345594|lt=Katsuragi, Wakayama|1=Katsuragi, Wakayama}}, it is a distinctive and valuable tradition unique to this region. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=Theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}
